items.question: Who still uses sigmoid activations in modern machine learning?
items.answer: Deep-learning practitioners apply sigmoid on binary output neurons, LSTM/GRU gates, and some probabilistic heads; classical ML uses the same σ in logistic regression. Hidden-layer usage is rare — ReLU family replaced sigmoid for depth.

items.question: What is the sigmoid activation function?
items.answer: σ(x) = 1/(1+e^(−x)), a smooth S-shaped map from any real x to the open interval (0, 1), with σ(0)=0.5 and derivative σ′(x)=σ(x)(1−σ(x))≤0.25.

items.question: When should you choose sigmoid over ReLU?
items.answer: When the layer must output a value strictly in (0, 1) — binary class probability, multiplicative gate, or bounded mixture weight — not when building deep hidden stacks where ReLU avoids saturation and vanishing gradients.

items.question: Where does saturation occur on the sigmoid curve?
items.answer: On the flat tails: x≫0 pushes σ(x)→1 with σ′(x)→0 (upper saturation); x≪0 pushes σ(x)→0 with σ′(x)→0 (lower saturation). The steepest, most trainable region is near x≈0.

items.question: Why do deep sigmoid hidden layers suffer vanishing gradients?
items.answer: Backpropagation multiplies per-layer factors σ′(x) each ≤0.25. In saturated regions σ′≈0, so the product shrinks exponentially with depth and early weights receive negligible updates.

items.question: How do you compute σ(x) and its derivative in code?
items.answer: Forward: σ(x)=1/(1+exp(−x)) with a stable branch for large |x|. Derivative: σ′(x)=σ(x)(1−σ(x)). In PyTorch, torch.sigmoid(x) for forward; frameworks fuse sigmoid+BCE as BCEWithLogitsLoss during training.

items.question: Sigmoid vs ReLU — when is each appropriate?
items.answer: Sigmoid: outputs in (0,1), good for binary probabilities and gates, but σ′≤0.25 causes vanishing gradients in deep hidden layers. ReLU: outputs [0,∞), gradient 1 for x>0, standard for deep hidden layers; not a probability map without extra machinery.

items.question: Sigmoid vs tanh — output range and optimization bias?
items.answer: Sigmoid maps to (0,1) — always positive activations, not zero-centered. Tanh maps to (−1,1), zero-centered, with max |tanh′|=1 versus sigmoid's 0.25. Both saturate at extremes; tanh was preferred over sigmoid in some hidden layers before ReLU, but neither is ideal for very deep stacks.

items.question: Sigmoid output vs raw logit — training implication?
items.answer: Logit z is unbounded and numerically stable inside BCEWithLogitsLoss. Pre-sigmoid probability ŷ=σ(z) saturates near 0/1, shrinking gradients and risking log(0) without clipping. Train on logits; apply σ only when probabilities are needed at inference.

items.question: Cause: Hidden pre-activation x=8 for many units across epochs. Effect?
items.answer: σ(8)≈0.9997 and σ′(8)≈0.0003, so backpropagated gradients to lower layers are scaled by ~10⁻³ per saturated unit — weights below learn slowly or stall while loss plateaus.

items.question: Cause: Replace sigmoid hidden layers with ReLU in a 10-layer MLP. Effect?
items.answer: Active neurons (x>0) pass gradient factor 1 instead of ≤0.25, so early-layer updates remain sizable and training converges faster; output layer can still use sigmoid or BCEWithLogitsLoss for binary probability.

items.question: What breaks if you stack 15 sigmoid hidden layers without normalization?
items.answer: Gradients to early layers shrink as ∏σ′(xₗ) with each factor ≤0.25 — often under 10⁻⁸ after many saturated layers. Training stalls or depends entirely on the last few layers; switching hidden activation to ReLU or adding normalization fixes the signal path.

items.question: What breaks if you feed sigmoid probabilities into BCELoss instead of logits?
items.answer: Gradients near ŷ≈0 or ŷ≈1 become extremely small (double saturation), loss values may be wrong if probabilities were rounded, and training slows dramatically compared to BCEWithLogitsLoss on raw logits.
